context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.
exercise:
Course context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.

Exercise:
When is it morally okay to kill one person so as to prevent five people from being killed? How would an act consequentialist think about this question? What would an act consequentialist say about the cases we considered in Lecture 13 (e.g. Fuel, Blood and the different trolley cases)? Does the act consequentialist get these cases right? If not, what is the right general account of when it is morally okay to kill one person so as to save five?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m really lost on the philosophy question about when it’s okay to kill one person to save five. I kind of get that act consequentialism says to look at outcomes, but then all the cases like Fuel, Blood, and the trolley ones start blending together for me.
exchanges.tutor:
Good—start by separating, not solving. For each case, ask only: what action is available, and how many live or die under each option?

If you apply that one test, would act consequentialism treat Fuel, Blood, and the trolley cases differently at all—or the same? Pick one case and tell me the options and outcomes in your own words.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will keep the response brief, use guided discovery, and avoid giving a direct solution. Since the student is confused by multiple cases blending together, I will reduce cognitive load by asking them to sort the cases using one clear comparison point that moves toward the assignment’s core: how act consequentialism evaluates actions by consequences. This supports the student in building their own framework before judging whether act consequentialism gets the cases right.

exchanges.student: That’s where I’m stuck, because once I do just outcomes, it feels like they all collapse into the same thing. Like if one dies either way but five are saved in one option, then I feel like act consequentialism would just say do that in all the cases, but I’m not sure if I’m oversimplifying.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s not oversimplifying; it may be the key pressure point. If only outcomes matter, then differences like pushing, redirecting, using, or withholding may not matter.

So ask yourself: does that mean act consequentialism gives the same verdict in all these cases? And if so, which case feels hardest to accept on that view—and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will affirm the student's useful insight without confirming it as the final answer, then push them to test it against the assignment's next step: whether act consequentialism treats the cases the same and whether that seems right. This keeps the discussion Socratic, focused, and moving toward a thesis structure without providing the conclusion for them.

exchanges.student: Yeah, I think that’s what’s bothering me. It seems like it would give basically the same verdict in all of them, but the pushing-type case feels way worse to me than just redirecting something, and I can’t explain why without getting muddled.
exchanges.tutor:
Good. Don’t explain all cases at once. Compare just two: redirecting vs pushing.

In one sentence each: in the redirect case, what role does the one person play in saving the five? In the pushing case, what role does that person play?

Focus on that difference, not yet on your final theory.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will help the student isolate the morally relevant contrast they intuit without supplying a theory or wording for them. A targeted comparison between two trolley-style cases will guide discovery toward distinctions like means/side-effect, intention, agency, or rights, while keeping the student doing the conceptual work. The response stays short and assignment-focused.

exchanges.student: I think in redirecting, the one person is sort of in the way after I change the track, but in pushing, the person is more like the thing I’m using to stop the trolley. I’m not sure if that distinction is actually philosophically real or if I’m just reacting emotionally.
exchanges.tutor:
That is a philosophically real distinction, not just a feeling. Now test it.

Ask: is the one person merely foreseen to die, or is that person’s death/body part of the plan for saving the five?

Try applying that test to Blood or Fuel. Which way does one of those cases come out, and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will validate the student's distinction as philosophically relevant without naming a full doctrine or giving the answer. Then I will push them to test whether the difference tracks a principle across cases, which advances the assignment from intuition to argument. This remains brief, Socratic, and avoids writing the student's paper for them.

exchanges.student: I’m getting mixed up again because “part of the plan” sounds kind of slippery to me. In Blood especially, I can’t tell if the person’s death is a side effect or if it’s being used, and then I start feeling like I need the whole lecture logic all over again.
exchanges.tutor:
Don’t replay the whole lecture. Use one test: if the one person did not die, could the five still be saved by that very plan?

If no, the death looks more like a means. If yes, it looks more like a side effect.

Try that on Blood. What does your answer seem to be?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is circling on a subtle distinction, so I should reduce complexity and avoid rehashing the whole lecture. I will give a concrete diagnostic question that helps them test 'part of the plan' without supplying conclusions. This keeps the response bite-sized, Socratic, and moves them toward applying a usable criterion across cases.

exchanges.student: I think my answer is no, but I’m not confident because I keep second-guessing what counts as “that very plan.” I feel like I sort of need the bigger picture again or I’m going to say something inconsistent.
exchanges.tutor:
You’re dwelling on one detail. Bigger picture: your paper likely needs 3 moves, not perfect certainty on every case.

1) What would act consequentialism say in general?
2) Which case makes that look implausible?
3) What kind of principle could explain the difference?

Give me a rough answer to each in one line.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is showing redundancy and getting stuck on a detail, so I should zoom out to the bigger structure of the assignment and ask them to organize their thinking into a simple framework. I will not solve the cases, but I will guide them to state the paper's three moves: act consequentialist verdict, where it seems wrong, and what principle might do better. This keeps the conversation on-track, bite-sized, and student-driven.

exchanges.student: I can try, but it still all feels blurry. I think act consequentialism says kill one if it saves five, then the pushing case makes that seem wrong, and then maybe the principle is something like you can’t use a person as a means, but I don’t really get how to make that fit the other cases.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s a workable core. Now pressure-test it instead of expanding it.

For each remaining case, ask: is the one person being used to bring about the rescue, or are they harmed as a byproduct of saving the five?

Pick just one—Fuel or Blood. Which side does it seem to fall on?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now articulated the core structure of a plausible argument. I should reinforce that progress without confirming a final answer, and push them to test their candidate principle against the remaining cases. To stay within the rules, I will not provide a solution or wording, but ask a focused comparative question that helps them refine consistency across Fuel, Blood, and trolley cases.

exchanges.student: I guess Blood seems like “used,” but then I immediately worry I’m labeling it that way just because I already decided pushing is bad. I’m having trouble telling whether I actually see the distinction or I’m just forcing the cases into it.
exchanges.tutor:
Good instinct—that’s exactly the right worry. A principle is weak if it’s just reverse-engineered from one case.

So test it two ways: did you choose the principle independently of the verdicts, and does it give consistent results across the cases?

Right now, where does your principle seem strongest, and where does it seem to strain?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is questioning whether their criterion is ad hoc, which is a productive philosophical concern. I should help them test for ad hoc reasoning by asking whether the principle was chosen independently and whether it generates verdicts they can accept across cases. This advances their critical evaluation without supplying conclusions, and remains concise.

exchanges.student: I think it seems strongest in pushing versus redirecting, because that difference feels clear to me. It strains in Blood, because I can’t tell if the person is genuinely being used or if I’m just making it sound that way after the fact.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s enough to work with. You do not need Blood to feel crystal clear; you need to say why it’s hard.

So isolate the ambiguity: what feature of Blood makes it look like a means, and what feature makes it look like a side effect?

Give one feature on each side.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified both a strong contrast and a problematic case. I should keep the answer short, acknowledge progress, and direct them to use the unclear case productively rather than aiming for perfect certainty. A focused question about what features of Blood make it ambiguous will help them generate analysis for the assignment without me supplying conclusions or wording.

exchanges.student: I think it looks like a means because the person’s harm seems tied pretty directly to how the five get saved. But it looks like a side effect because I’m not always sure the rescue literally depends on using that person, instead of their death just happening as part of the process.
exchanges.tutor:
Good. That’s the core tension. Don’t chase certainty now—use the tension as analysis.

Next step: state your view in 3 short claims:
1) what act consequentialism says,
2) why that seems wrong in at least one case,
3) what your alternative principle tries to capture, even if Blood stays tricky.

Write those 3 claims in your own words.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced the needed distinction. I should reinforce the progress, keep them from overthinking, and push them to synthesize their own argument structure without writing it for them. To obey the rules, I will not provide polished language or a solution; I will ask them to assemble a short claim sequence that can become their draft.
